The blast radius of an undetected NTLM Type 3 leak EXCEEDS a Basic Auth leak in the worst case — NTLM enables relay attacks even WITHOUT cracking the password, while a Basic Auth leak requires the attacker to actually use the recovered `user:password` pair.

**Sites closed (1 detector regex + 1 table entry):**

  * `src/utils/secret_scanner.py:_NTLM_RE` — new regex appended to `_AUTH_SCHEME_DETECTORS`:
    ```python
    _NTLM_RE = re.compile(r"(?i)NTLM\s+([A-Za-z0-9+/=]{50,})")
    _AUTH_SCHEME_DETECTORS: tuple[tuple[re.Pattern[str], str], ...] = (
        (_BEARER_RE, "Bearer-Token wirkt echt"),
        (_BASIC_AUTH_RE, "HTTP Basic Authentication Credential gefunden"),
        (_NEGOTIATE_RE, "SPNEGO/Negotiate Authentication Token gefunden"),
        (_NTLM_RE, "NTLM Authentication Credential gefunden"),
    )
    ```
    The body alphabet `[A-Za-z0-9+/=]` is the canonical base64 alphabet per RFC 4648 §4 (standard base64; NTLMSSP messages always use standard base64 in HTTP Authorization headers per [MS-NLMP] §2.2 — the binary protocol is rendered into the textual HTTP scheme via standard base64, never the URL-safe `_-` variant). The 50+ char body floor is the structural disambiguator — real NTLMSSP messages are ALL above 50 chars (Type 1 = 40 bytes raw = 56 base64 chars minimum; Type 2 = 80-200 bytes raw = 108-272 base64 chars; Type 3 = 350-1000+ bytes raw = 470-1500+ base64 chars), so 50 is a safe conservative floor that catches every realistic NTLMSSP message including truncated Type 1 tokens in fragmented log lines. The acronym `NTLM` doesn't appear in natural English prose followed by 50+ contiguous chars from the base64 alphabet (English words break at whitespace and punctuation). The `(?i)` inline flag inherits the RFC 7235 §2.1 case-insensitivity contract from the Bearer / Basic / Negotiate siblings.

  * `_AUTH_SCHEME_DETECTORS` table extension — the canonical `_scan_auth_scheme_credentials` helper (single source of truth for HTTP-auth-scheme detector processing, extracted by the 2026-05-16 Basic Auth round) processes the new entry uniformly via the existing iteration loop with the same `_looks_like_secret(candidate, is_assignment=True)` filter (`min_categories=1` so uniform-character-class bodies are NOT silently undetected) and `covered_ranges` mutation contract. No changes needed to `_scan_content` (C901 complexity unchanged) or to `_scan_auth_scheme_credentials` (no new branches). The structural invariant established by the 2026-05-16 Basic Auth round and confirmed by the 2026-05-16 Negotiate round held end-to-end for the FOURTH HTTP auth-scheme detector: adding a new HTTP-auth-scheme detector requires only a new regex constant + a new tuple entry — the canonical processing helper handles it without modification.

**Severity:** **MEDIUM-HIGH** — detection-from-zero failure for a class of realistic NTLM credential leaks in enterprise Windows environments (Active Directory, IIS legacy auth, WinRM, SharePoint, SMB-over-HTTP, WebDAV). The high-severity case is the silent-undetection branch (all-letter bodies): the NTLMSSP message (potentially Type 3 with a NetNTLMv2 hash recoverable to the plaintext password via offline cracking) sits committed in plaintext with NO detection at all. The attribution-drift branch is medium-severity (the body IS caught generically, but the NTLM-specific recovery surface that determines which IR playbook applies — rotate user password, audit domain controller for NetNTLMv2 relay attempts via `ntlmrelayx`, force user re-auth, offline-crack the hash with `hashcat` mode 5600 to recover plaintext — is lost). Mitigated only by the requirement that the leaked credential live alongside an `NTLM` auth-scheme literal in the same content blob; in practice this covers every leak through IIS debug logs, browser HAR exports of intranet sites, Wireshark/tshark SMB-over-HTTP captures, WinRM diagnostics, and curl/requests-ntlm debug output. CodeQL / Semgrep do not flag this drift because their default rules cover Bearer / Basic auth-schemes but not direct-NTLM (NTLM-specific detection requires a per-rule body-alphabet and body-length disambiguator, which is rule-table dependent — no industry-standard default).

**Fix:** Two-part change:
1. Add `_NTLM_RE = re.compile(r"(?i)NTLM\s+([A-Za-z0-9+/=]{50,})")` alongside `_BEARER_RE`, `_BASIC_AUTH_RE`, and `_NEGOTIATE_RE` (single source of truth for the auth-scheme detector body shape).
2. Append `(_NTLM_RE, "NTLM Authentication Credential gefunden")` to `_AUTH_SCHEME_DETECTORS` — the canonical iteration helper `_scan_auth_scheme_credentials` processes the new entry uniformly. No code changes elsewhere — the helper preserves byte-for-byte behaviour of the Bearer/Basic/Negotiate detectors while extending coverage to direct-NTLM.

Zero false-positive risk: the body alphabet `[A-Za-z0-9+/=]` is identical to `_BASIC_AUTH_RE`'s and `_NEGOTIATE_RE`'s (no `-_.` like Bearer's URL-safe-ish `token68` ABNF); the structural disambiguator (`\s+` between the auth-scheme literal and a 50+ char body) is identical to Negotiate's. The acronym `NTLM` is more selective than the English word `Negotiate` for natural-language false positives (Negotiate is a real English verb commonly appearing in prose; NTLM is a Microsoft technical acronym that almost never appears outside technical contexts). The `_looks_like_secret(candidate, is_assignment=True)` filter at the second layer (`min_categories=1`) catches any token-shaped string that does happen to follow an `ntlm`-prefixed natural-language passage — the same defence Bearer/Basic/Negotiate already rely on.

**Tests added:** `tests/test_sentinel_secret_scanner_ntlm_drift.py` — 39 tests across 10 categories:
  1. Per-case attribution PoCs (5 case variants × 240-char NTLMSSP-prefixed long-mixed body) — proves attribution-drift closes for every RFC-7235-conforming NTLM literal.
  2. Per-case silent-undetection PoCs (4 case variants × 62-char all-letter body) — proves `candidate.isalpha()` skip bypass closes for NTLM-prefixed tokens.
  3. Negative cases (13 natural-language passages, code-shape candidates, and a 49-char body just-below-floor) — proves false-positive surface is bounded.
  4. Cross-detector ordering regression guard — proves JWT in Bearer header still yields JWT attribution (the `_KNOWN_TOKENS` matching runs BEFORE auth-scheme detectors).
  5. Three regression guards — proves canonical Bearer, Basic Auth, and Negotiate continue to detect after the NTLM addition (the FOURTH HTTP auth-scheme detector in `_AUTH_SCHEME_DETECTORS`).
  6. Compiled-regex invariant — pins `_NTLM_RE.flags & re.IGNORECASE` programmatically against future case-sensitivity regression.
  7. Auth-scheme detector table membership invariant — pins `_NTLM_RE` membership in `_AUTH_SCHEME_DETECTORS` so a future regression that adds the regex but forgets the table binding fails fast.
  8. End-to-end emission-shape inventory (9 real-world emission patterns: curl debug logs, IIS debug logs, browser HAR exports, Python/JSON fixtures, YAML config, WinRM debug traces, Wireshark text exports, WWW-Authenticate response headers).
  9. Masking-contract test — proves the raw base64 NTLMSSP body never appears unmasked in finding output (mirrors Bearer/Basic/Negotiate masking-contract tests).
 10. NTLMSSP Type 1 structural-prefix demonstration documenting the canonical `TlRMTVNTUA` base64 prefix (base64 of the ASCII `NTLMSSP\0` magic per [MS-NLMP] §2.2 Header) as an incident-response anchor distinguishing direct-NTLM from Kerberos AP-REQ (the `YII` prefix from the 2026-05-16 Negotiate round).

**Learning:** The HTTP-auth-scheme detector family extends BEYOND IETF-RFC-defined schemes to include vendor-specific HTTP auth-scheme literals ([MS-NLMP] NTLM) — the structural invariant ("HTTP-auth-scheme literals go to `_AUTH_SCHEME_DETECTORS` regardless of their underlying spec body") proved end-to-end for the FOURTH detector. The 2026-05-16 Basic Auth round's `_AUTH_SCHEME_DETECTORS` tuple + `_scan_auth_scheme_credentials` helper architecture continues to hold byte-for-byte: adding a vendor-defined auth-scheme (NTLM per [MS-NLMP]) required ONLY a new regex constant + a new tuple entry. ZERO changes to the iteration helper, ZERO changes to `_scan_content`, ZERO C901 complexity impact. **The body-length floor remains the per-scheme structural disambiguator** — NTLM uses 50+ chars (matches truncated Type 1 messages at 56 base64 chars while excluding the technical acronym in prose). **The body alphabet remains determined by the spec's encoding** — direct-NTLM, like Basic (RFC 7617) and Negotiate (RFC 4559), uses standard base64 `[A-Za-z0-9+/=]` per [MS-NLMP] §2.2 (the binary NTLMSSP wire format rendered to textual HTTP via standard base64). **The classification question — "vendor literal in `_AUTH_SCHEME_DETECTORS` or `_KNOWN_TOKENS`?" — resolves to the auth-scheme table** when the literal IS an HTTP auth-scheme (the wire-protocol position determines placement, NOT the spec authoring body). NTLM is an HTTP auth-scheme literal that happens to be defined in a Microsoft spec instead of an IETF RFC; the placement is in `_AUTH_SCHEME_DETECTORS` for uniformity with Bearer/Basic/Negotiate.

**Next-round candidates (named-but-deferred):** **GitHub `Token` scheme detector** (`(?i)token\s+[A-Za-z0-9_\-]{36,}`) — GitHub's older API alias for Bearer (`Authorization: token ghp_...`); the embedded GitHub PAT (`ghp_`-prefixed) already matches `_KNOWN_TOKENS` via the `ghp_` regex, so the `token` scheme detector is attribution-only for opaque non-GitHub tokens that happen to use the alias. **HOBA detector** (`(?i)HOBA\s+...`) — RFC 7486 HTTP Origin-Bound Auth; rare in practice but a complete auth-scheme enumeration would include it. **Mutual auth detector** (`(?i)Mutual\s+...`) — RFC 8120 HTTP Mutual Authentication using cryptographic keys; very rare. **Digest Auth detector** (`(?i)Digest\s+username=...`) — RFC 7616 multi-field challenge-response auth STILL deferred pending body-structure-aware detector framework (the credential is split across the `nonce` / `response` / `cnonce` fields, so a single-regex anchor doesn't fully capture the secret — context-aware extraction needed; this remains the longest-deferred candidate from the auth-scheme family because the architectural lift is meaningfully larger than the per-detector additions of the past four rounds). **AWS4-HMAC-SHA256 detector** (`(?i)AWS4-HMAC-SHA256\s+Credential=...`) — AWS Sig v4 signed-request auth-scheme; the body is multi-field (`Credential` / `SignedHeaders` / `Signature`) like Digest, so similarly deferred pending body-structure-aware framework.

**Marker:** SENTINEL_NTLM_DRIFT.

## 2026-05-16 - SPNEGO/Negotiate Detector Drift (RFC 4559 HTTP SPNEGO Authentication, Sibling Of Basic Auth Round): The Adjacent-Detector Candidate Named-But-Deferred By The 2026-05-16 Basic Auth Round Closed In Full — A Leaked `Authorization: Negotiate <base64(GSSAPI-token)>` In Committed Source Carried Two Orthogonal Failure Modes (Attribution Drift For 200+ Char Kerberos Tokens, Silent Undetection For All-Letter Bodies) That Lost The Kerberos-Specific Revocation Playbook (KDC Service Ticket Revocation, Force User Re-Auth, Audit Service Principal For Replay Within 8-10h Ticket Lifetime) Entirely

**Vulnerability:** The 2026-05-16 Basic Auth round (`security(secret-scanner): add Basic Auth detector per RFC 7617`) named **SPNEGO Negotiate detector** as the second-priority adjacent-detector candidate (after the deferred Digest Auth) sharing the same `(?i)` case-insensitivity contract on the auth-scheme literal per RFC 7235 §2.1 (referenced by RFC 4559 §4 — HTTP SPNEGO Authentication for Kerberos and NTLMSSP via GSSAPI). Pre-fix `src/utils/secret_scanner.py` had NO Negotiate detector at all — the only coverage for a leaked `Authorization: Negotiate <body>` was via the generic entropy fallback (`_HIGH_ENTROPY_RE` = `(?<![A-Za-z0-9])[A-Za-z0-9+/=_-]{24,}(?![A-Za-z0-9])`) which matched the base64-encoded GSSAPI body generically, BUT only when the body had ≥2 character categories AND was not all-letter (the `candidate.isalpha()` short-circuit in `_HIGH_ENTROPY_RE`'s loop suppresses LongCamelCaseClassName false positives). Two orthogonal failure modes resulted: **(1) Attribution drift** — 200+ char Kerberos AP-REQ / AS-REP base64 bodies with mixed character classes (the common case — ASN.1 DER envelope + encrypted ticket combines digits, upper, lower, `+/=`) lost the SPNEGO-specific attribution and landed as generic `Hochentropischer Token-String`, so incident-response triage had to GUESS the revocation playbook (SPNEGO/Kerberos: revoke the service ticket at the KDC via Kerberos `kadmin` / Active Directory `setspn`, force user re-authentication via `klist purge` + `kinit`, audit the affected service principal for replay activity within the ticket's typical 8-10h lifetime per RFC 4120 §5.3 `EndTime` default; Bearer: revoke the opaque token at the issuing IdP; Basic Auth: rotate the user's password; opaque API key: revoke at the vendor dashboard — four entirely different IR flows that hinge on per-scheme attribution). **(2) Silent undetection** — all-letter base64 bodies trip `candidate.isalpha()` and are skipped by the entropy fallback. Pre-fix every leaked `Negotiate <all-letter-body>` was SILENTLY UNDETECTED — the CI gate passed, the encrypted Kerberos ticket sat committed in the public repo for the full ticket validity window (8-10h). End-to-end PoC verified pre-fix across every case variation (`negotiate` / `NEGOTIATE` / `NeGoTiAtE` / `nEgOtIaTe` / canonical `Negotiate`) and every body class (long-mixed 240-char, all-letter 62-char, canonical `YII`-prefixed Kerberos AP-REQ shape): only `Verdächtige Zuweisung eines potentiellen Secrets` or `Hochentropischer Token-String` fired (when the variable name happened to carry a sensitive keyword or the body was mixed-class above 24 chars), losing all SPNEGO-specific attribution.

**Threat model:** A leaked SPNEGO/Negotiate credential is structurally distinct from Bearer/Basic Auth leaks in that **the body is a base64-encoded ASN.1-DER GSSAPI token wrapping either a Kerberos AS-REP / AP-REQ message or an NTLMSSP Type 1/2/3 message** per RFC 4178 (SPNEGO) and [MS-NLMP] (NTLMSSP). An attacker who recovers a Negotiate body therefore holds: (a) **the encrypted service ticket** — replayable as the original user against the target service for the ticket's validity window (typical 8-10h KDC default per RFC 4120 §5.3); the 5-minute authenticator clock-skew check (RFC 4120 §3.2) does not fully mitigate replay on short timescales; (b) **the realm name** — the ASN.1 `realm` field is unencrypted in the AS-REQ message and identifies the target Kerberos realm for further attacks; (c) **the SPNEGO mech-types** — identifies the available authentication mechanisms (Kerberos / NTLMSSP / NEGOEX) at the target, helping attackers pivot to the weakest available; (d) **AS-REP roasting amplifier** — if the leaked token contains an AS-REP message with encrypted PA-DATA (less common but possible during error/debug logging when pre-authentication is disabled), the encrypted portion can be subjected to offline dictionary/brute-force attack with tools like `hashcat` (mode 18200 for AS-REP / 19600 for TGS-REP); (e) **NTLMSSP exposure** — when the Negotiate envelope wraps an NTLMSSP message, NTLMv2 responses leaked in Type 3 messages are vulnerable to NetNTLMv2 relay attacks (using `responder` / `ntlmrelayx`) and offline cracking with `hashcat` (mode 5600). Real-world emission patterns include IIS HTTP request logs with `--debug` flag (the entire Authorization header is logged verbatim per `UseAppPoolQueueLength` diagnostic mode), browser dev-tools Network tab exports (`Save HAR with content` captures the Authorization header verbatim into the HAR JSON), Wireshark/tshark capture exports rendered as text, WinRM debug logs (`Set-PSDebug -Trace 2`) emitting the Authorization header during the negotiation round trip, `curl -v --negotiate -u :` debug logs, Python `requests` with `requests-kerberos` debug mode (`logging.DEBUG` on the `requests_kerberos` logger), Spring Security `org.springframework.security.kerberos` debug logging, and ELK Stack ingest of `WWW-Authenticate` response headers. The blast radius of an undetected Negotiate leak is COMPARABLE to a Basic Auth leak in the worst case (full Kerberos credential exposure via AS-REP roasting recovers the user's password hash, which can be cracked offline to recover the plaintext password — feeding the same password-reuse amplifier chain).

**Sites closed (1 detector regex + 1 table entry):**

  * `src/utils/secret_scanner.py:_NEGOTIATE_RE` — new regex appended to `_AUTH_SCHEME_DETECTORS`:
    ```python
    _NEGOTIATE_RE = re.compile(r"(?i)Negotiate\s+([A-Za-z0-9+/=]{50,})")
    _AUTH_SCHEME_DETECTORS: tuple[tuple[re.Pattern[str], str], ...] = (
        (_BEARER_RE, "Bearer-Token wirkt echt"),
        (_BASIC_AUTH_RE, "HTTP Basic Authentication Credential gefunden"),
        (_NEGOTIATE_RE, "SPNEGO/Negotiate Authentication Token gefunden"),
    )
    ```
    The body alphabet `[A-Za-z0-9+/=]` is the canonical base64 alphabet per RFC 4648 §4 (standard base64; RFC 4559 §4 references the original base64 encoding per RFC 1421, which uses `+/=` not URL-safe `_-`). The 50+ char body floor is the structural disambiguator — real Kerberos AS-REP / AP-REQ tokens are 200-3000+ chars base64-encoded (the ASN.1 DER envelope alone is ~100 bytes, the encrypted ticket adds 200-2000+ bytes for typical service principals), so 50 is a safe conservative floor that catches even truncated tokens in fragmented log lines while preventing natural-language false positives (the English word "negotiate" appears commonly in code comments and prose, but never followed by 50+ contiguous chars from `[A-Za-z0-9+/=]` because English words break at whitespace and punctuation). The `(?i)` inline flag inherits the RFC 7235 §2.1 case-insensitivity contract from the Bearer/Basic siblings.

  * `_AUTH_SCHEME_DETECTORS` table extension — the canonical `_scan_auth_scheme_credentials` helper extracted by the 2026-05-16 Basic Auth round (single source of truth for HTTP-auth-scheme detector processing) processes the new entry uniformly via the existing iteration loop with the same `_looks_like_secret(candidate, is_assignment=True)` filter (`min_categories=1` so uniform-character-class bodies are NOT silently undetected) and `covered_ranges` mutation contract. No changes needed to `_scan_content` (C901 complexity unchanged at 29) or to `_scan_auth_scheme_credentials` (no new branches). The structural invariant established by the 2026-05-16 Basic Auth round held end-to-end: adding a new HTTP-auth-scheme detector requires only a new regex constant + a new tuple entry — the canonical processing helper handles it without modification.

**Severity:** **MEDIUM-HIGH** — detection-from-zero failure for a class of realistic SPNEGO/Kerberos credential leaks in enterprise environments (Active Directory, IIS, WinRM, Spring Security with Kerberos integration). The high-severity case is the silent-undetection branch (all-letter bodies): the entire encrypted Kerberos ticket sits committed in plaintext with NO detection at all for the full ticket validity window (8-10h). The attribution-drift branch is medium-severity (the body IS caught generically, but the SPNEGO-specific reason that determines which IR playbook applies — KDC service ticket revocation via `kadmin` / Active Directory `setspn`, force user re-auth via `klist purge` + `kinit`, audit service principal for replay activity — is lost). Mitigated only by the requirement that the leaked credential live alongside a `Negotiate` auth-scheme literal in the same content blob; in practice this covers every leak through IIS debug logs, browser HAR exports, Wireshark/tshark captures, WinRM diagnostics, and curl/requests-kerberos debug output. CodeQL / Semgrep do not flag this drift because their default rules cover the Bearer auth-scheme but not Negotiate (the canonical Negotiate detector requires a per-rule body-alphabet and body-length disambiguator, which is rule-table dependent — no industry-standard default).

**Fix:** Two-part change:
1. Add `_NEGOTIATE_RE = re.compile(r"(?i)Negotiate\s+([A-Za-z0-9+/=]{50,})")` alongside `_BEARER_RE` and `_BASIC_AUTH_RE` (single source of truth for the auth-scheme detector body shape).
2. Append `(_NEGOTIATE_RE, "SPNEGO/Negotiate Authentication Token gefunden")` to `_AUTH_SCHEME_DETECTORS` — the canonical iteration helper `_scan_auth_scheme_credentials` (extracted by the 2026-05-16 Basic Auth round as a one-time refactor) processes the new entry uniformly. No code changes elsewhere — the helper preserves byte-for-byte behaviour of the Bearer/Basic detectors while extending coverage to Negotiate.

Zero false-positive risk: the body alphabet `[A-Za-z0-9+/=]` is identical to `_BASIC_AUTH_RE`'s (no `-_.` like Bearer); the structural disambiguator (`\s+` between the auth-scheme literal and a 50+ char body) is identical in shape to Basic's (and Bearer's, both at 16+ chars — Negotiate raised to 50+ because real GSSAPI tokens are massively longer and the higher floor further suppresses already-rare natural-language false positives). Natural-language false positives are bounded by the same logic that protects Bearer/Basic: an English sentence containing "Negotiate" cannot produce 50+ contiguous chars from the base64 alphabet because words are separated by whitespace. The `_looks_like_secret(candidate, is_assignment=True)` filter at the second layer (`min_categories=1`) catches any token-shaped string that does happen to follow a `negotiate`-prefixed natural-language passage — the same defence Bearer/Basic already rely on.

**Tests added:** `tests/test_sentinel_secret_scanner_negotiate_drift.py` — 36 tests across 10 categories:
  1. Per-case attribution PoCs (5 case variants × 240-char long-mixed body) — proves attribution-drift closes for every RFC-7235-conforming Negotiate literal.
  2. Per-case silent-undetection PoCs (4 case variants × 62-char all-letter body) — proves `candidate.isalpha()` skip bypass closes for Negotiate-prefixed tokens.
  3. Negative cases (12 natural-language passages, code-shape candidates, and a 49-char body just-below-floor) — proves false-positive surface is bounded.
  4. Cross-detector ordering regression guard — proves JWT in Bearer header still yields JWT attribution (the `_KNOWN_TOKENS` matching runs BEFORE auth-scheme detectors).
  5. Bearer regression guard — proves canonical Bearer continues to detect after the Negotiate addition.
  6. Basic Auth regression guard — proves canonical Basic Auth (the 2026-05-16 sibling) continues to detect after the Negotiate addition.
  7. Compiled-regex invariant — pins `_NEGOTIATE_RE.flags & re.IGNORECASE` programmatically against future case-sensitivity regression.
  8. Auth-scheme detector table membership invariant — pins `_NEGOTIATE_RE` membership in `_AUTH_SCHEME_DETECTORS` so a future regression that adds the regex but forgets the table binding fails fast.
  9. End-to-end emission-shape inventory (8 real-world emission patterns: curl debug logs, IIS debug logs, browser HAR exports, Python/JSON fixtures, YAML config, WinRM debug traces, Wireshark text exports).
 10. Masking-contract test — proves the raw base64 GSSAPI body never appears unmasked in finding output (mirrors Bearer/Basic masking-contract tests). PLUS a Kerberos AP-REQ structural-prefix demonstration documenting the canonical `YII` base64 prefix (base64 of the ASN.1 SEQUENCE outer tag `0x60 0x82`) as an incident-response anchor for distinguishing Kerberos from NTLMSSP-wrapped Negotiate tokens (NTLMSSP base64 starts with `TlRMTVNTUA` = base64("NTLMSSP\0")).

**Learning:** The HTTP-auth-scheme detector family is a closed enumeration per the RFC 7235 §2.1 case-insensitivity contract. The 2026-05-16 Basic Auth round's `_AUTH_SCHEME_DETECTORS` tuple + `_scan_auth_scheme_credentials` helper architecture proved end-to-end: adding a third RFC-defined auth-scheme (Negotiate per RFC 4559) required ONLY a new regex constant + a new tuple entry. ZERO changes to the iteration helper, ZERO changes to `_scan_content`, ZERO C901 complexity impact. The structural invariant pinned by the Basic Auth round held byte-for-byte. **The body-length floor is the per-scheme structural disambiguator** — Bearer/Basic use 16+ chars (matches short Basic Auth credentials like base64("admin:password") = 20 chars), Negotiate uses 50+ chars (matches truncated GSSAPI tokens while excluding natural-language false positives where the English word "negotiate" might be followed by short identifier names). Future auth-scheme detectors choose the floor per the minimum realistic credential length for that scheme's RFC-defined structure. **Per-scheme body alphabet** is determined by the RFC's body encoding — Bearer (RFC 6750) allows `[A-Za-z0-9\-_.]` per `token68` ABNF (URL-safe-ish), Basic (RFC 7617) and Negotiate (RFC 4559 referencing RFC 1421) use standard base64 `[A-Za-z0-9+/=]`. Future auth-scheme detectors pick the body alphabet per the RFC.

**Next-round candidates (named-but-deferred):** **NTLM detector** (`(?i)NTLM\s+[A-Za-z0-9+/=]{50,}`) — Microsoft NTLM directly as an HTTP auth-scheme (without SPNEGO wrapping); used by IIS for legacy clients and SMB-over-HTTP scenarios. The literal `NTLM` is vendor-specific per [MS-NLMP] (not IETF-RFC-defined like Negotiate), creating a structural classification question: belongs to `_AUTH_SCHEME_DETECTORS` (because it IS an HTTP auth-scheme literal) OR `_KNOWN_TOKENS` (because [MS-NLMP] is a vendor spec, not an IETF RFC)? Per the structural invariant established by the Basic Auth round ("HTTP-auth-scheme literals go to `_AUTH_SCHEME_DETECTORS`"), NTLM should go in the auth-scheme table — the canonical processing path already handles it without modification. **Digest Auth detector** (`(?i)Digest\s+username=...`) — RFC 7616 multi-field challenge-response auth still deferred pending body-structure-aware detector framework (the credential is split across the `nonce` / `response` / `cnonce` fields, so a single-regex anchor doesn't fully capture the secret — context-aware extraction needed). **GitHub `Token` scheme detector** (`(?i)token\s+[A-Za-z0-9_\-]{36,}`) — GitHub's older API alias for Bearer (`Authorization: token ghp_...`); the embedded GitHub PAT (`ghp_`-prefixed) already matches `_KNOWN_TOKENS` via the `ghp_` regex, so the `token` scheme detector is attribution-only for opaque non-GitHub tokens that happen to use the alias. **HOBA detector** (`(?i)HOBA\s+...`) — RFC 7486 HTTP Origin-Bound Auth; rare in practice but a complete auth-scheme enumeration would include it. **Mutual auth detector** (`(?i)Mutual\s+...`) — RFC 8120 HTTP Mutual Authentication using cryptographic keys; very rare.

**Marker:** SENTINEL_NEGOTIATE_DRIFT.

## 2026-05-16 - Basic Auth Detector Drift (RFC 7617 HTTP Basic Authentication, Sibling Of Bearer Case-Insensitivity Round): The Adjacent-Detector Candidate Named-But-Deferred By The 2026-05-15 Bearer Case-Insensitivity Round Closed In Full — A Leaked `Authorization: Basic <base64(user:password)>` In Committed Source Carried Two Orthogonal Failure Modes (Attribution Drift For 24+ Char Bodies, Silent Undetection For Short Or All-Letter Bodies) That Lost The Plaintext `username:password` Recovery Surface Entirely

**Vulnerability:** The 2026-05-15 Bearer case-insensitivity round (`security(secret-scanner): pin Bearer detector to case-insensitive per RFC 7235`) named **Basic Auth detector** as the immediate adjacent-detector candidate sharing the same `(?i)` case-insensitivity contract on the auth-scheme literal per RFC 7235 §2.1 (referenced by RFC 7617 §2 — HTTP Basic Authentication). Pre-fix `src/utils/secret_scanner.py` had NO Basic Auth detector at all — the only coverage for a leaked `Authorization: Basic <body>` was via the generic entropy fallback (`_HIGH_ENTROPY_RE` = `(?<![A-Za-z0-9])[A-Za-z0-9+/=_-]{24,}(?![A-Za-z0-9])`) which matched the base64-encoded body span generically, BUT only when the body was long enough (24+ chars) AND contained ≥2 character categories AND was not all-letter (the `candidate.isalpha()` short-circuit in `_HIGH_ENTROPY_RE`'s loop suppresses LongCamelCaseClassName false positives). Two orthogonal failure modes resulted: **(1) Attribution drift** — 24+ char base64 bodies with mixed character classes lost the Basic-Auth-specific attribution and landed as generic `Hochentropischer Token-String`, so incident-response triage had to GUESS the revocation playbook (Basic Auth: rotate the user's password AND audit downstream systems where the same password may be reused via password-reuse amplification; Bearer: revoke the opaque token at the issuing IdP; opaque API key: revoke at the vendor dashboard). **(2) Silent undetection** — short bodies (16-23 chars, e.g. `YWRtaW46cGFzc3dvcmQ=` = `admin:password` is only 20 chars) fall BELOW the entropy fallback's 24-char minimum entirely; all-letter base64 bodies trip `candidate.isalpha()` and are skipped by the entropy fallback. Pre-fix every leaked `Basic <short-or-all-letter-body>` was SILENTLY UNDETECTED — the CI gate passed, the plaintext `username:password` pair (trivially recovered via `base64.b64decode(body).decode()`) sat committed in the public repo indefinitely. End-to-end PoC verified pre-fix across every case variation (`basic` / `BASIC` / `BaSiC` / `bAsIc` / canonical `Basic`) and every body class (short, long-mixed, all-letter): only `Verdächtige Zuweisung eines potentiellen Secrets` fired (and only when the variable name happened to carry a sensitive keyword), losing all Basic-Auth-specific attribution.

**Threat model:** A leaked Basic Auth credential carries whatever scope the issuing service grants the `username:password` pair. The structurally critical distinction from Bearer Auth is that **the body is a reversible base64 encoding of the cleartext `username:password` string** — `base64.b64decode("YWRtaW46cGFzc3dvcmQ=").decode("ascii")` returns `"admin:password"`. An attacker who recovers a Basic Auth body therefore holds (a) the username (which may be a system administrator account, service account, or canonical project owner — directly identifying the leaking entity), (b) the plaintext password (which may be reused across other systems by the same user — the canonical real-world IR-amplifier for credential leaks), and (c) by inference, the existence of an HTTP-Basic-Auth-protected service that the credential pair authenticates to. Real-world emission patterns include curl `-u user:pass` debug logs (Basic Auth is curl's default `--user` shape), httpie `--auth=user:pass` debug logs, ASP.NET / Spring Boot / Django Basic Auth Authorization headers committed to test fixtures, hand-typed examples in documentation or migration guides, and hostile-PR fragments mimicking existing canonical-case detector evasion. The blast radius of an undetected Basic Auth leak is HIGHER than an undetected Bearer leak: a leaked Bearer is opaque so the attacker can only use it as-is until revocation, but a leaked Basic Auth provides the *cleartext password* that may be reused across other systems by the same user (password reuse is the dominant real-world account-takeover amplifier per every published breach analysis: LinkedIn 2012, Adobe 2013, Yahoo 2013/2014, Equifax 2017, the Marriott Starwood 2018 chain).

**Sites closed (1 detector regex + 1 helper extraction):**

  * `src/utils/secret_scanner.py:_BASIC_AUTH_RE` — new regex tuple appended to a new `_AUTH_SCHEME_DETECTORS` table:
    ```python
    _BASIC_AUTH_RE = re.compile(r"(?i)Basic\s+([A-Za-z0-9+/=]{16,})")
    _AUTH_SCHEME_DETECTORS: tuple[tuple[re.Pattern[str], str], ...] = (
        (_BEARER_RE, "Bearer-Token wirkt echt"),
        (_BASIC_AUTH_RE, "HTTP Basic Authentication Credential gefunden"),
    )
    ```
    The body alphabet `[A-Za-z0-9+/=]` is the canonical base64 alphabet per RFC 4648 §4 (standard base64; URL-safe RFC 4648 §5 uses `_-` instead of `+/` but is rare in Authorization headers per RFC 7617 §2 which prescribes standard base64). The 16+ contiguous body length is the structural disambiguator against natural-language false positives — sentences like "Basic understanding of..." do NOT have 16+ contiguous chars from the base64 alphabet following the literal (English words break at spaces and punctuation). The `(?i)` inline flag inherits the RFC 7235 §2.1 case-insensitivity contract from the Bearer round.

  * `src/utils/secret_scanner.py:_scan_auth_scheme_credentials` — new helper extracted from `_scan_content` to keep that function at its existing C901 complexity baseline (29) while still adding the new detector. The helper iterates `_AUTH_SCHEME_DETECTORS` uniformly, applies the same `_looks_like_secret(candidate, is_assignment=True)` filter (`min_categories=1`) that the original Bearer loop used (so uniform-character-class bodies are NOT silently undetected), and mutates `covered_ranges` in place so subsequent detector loops correctly suppress the same span. Future detectors (RFC 7616 Digest, RFC 4559 SPNEGO, RFC 7486 HOBA) extend `_AUTH_SCHEME_DETECTORS` without changing the helper or `_scan_content` — single point of audit for every HTTP-auth-scheme detector.

**Severity:** **MEDIUM-HIGH** — detection-from-zero failure for a class of realistic Basic Auth credential leaks. The high-severity case is the silent-undetection branch (short bodies < 24 chars OR all-letter bodies): the entire `username:password` pair sits committed in plaintext with NO detection at all. The attribution-drift branch is medium-severity (the body IS caught generically, but the Basic-Auth-specific reason that determines which rotation playbook applies — rotate the user's password, audit password-reuse cross-system — is lost). Mitigated only by the requirement that the leaked credential live alongside a `Basic` auth-scheme literal in the same content blob; in practice this covers every leak through automation-generated fixtures, lowercase-normalising loggers, hostile-PR-introduced fragments, and curl/httpie debug logs. CodeQL / Semgrep do not flag this drift because their default rules cover the Bearer auth-scheme but not Basic (the canonical Basic detector requires a per-rule body-alphabet disambiguator, which is rule-table dependent — no industry-standard default).

**Fix:** Three-part change:
1. Add `_BASIC_AUTH_RE = re.compile(r"(?i)Basic\s+([A-Za-z0-9+/=]{16,})")` alongside `_BEARER_RE` (single source of truth for the auth-scheme detector body shape).
2. Add `_AUTH_SCHEME_DETECTORS` tuple binding each regex to its Basic-Auth-specific reason — placing both detectors in a single iterable so future auth-scheme additions (Digest, SPNEGO, HOBA) follow the same shape without modifying `_scan_content`.
3. Extract `_scan_auth_scheme_credentials(content, covered_ranges, line_resolver)` helper from the inline Bearer loop in `_scan_content`. The helper preserves the original Bearer detector's behaviour byte-for-byte (same `_looks_like_secret` filter, same `is_covered` semantics, same mutation of `covered_ranges`) while routing both detectors through one canonical implementation. The extraction REDUCES `_scan_content`'s C901 complexity by 2 (eliminates one inline detector loop) while ADDING the Basic detector — net cyclomatic complexity at the function level: unchanged (still 29, matches `.c901-baseline.txt`).

Zero false-positive risk: the body alphabet `[A-Za-z0-9+/=]` is strictly narrower than `_BEARER_RE`'s `[A-Za-z0-9\-_.]` (no `-`, no `_`, no `.`); the structural disambiguator (`\s+` between the auth-scheme literal and a 16+ char body) is identical to Bearer's. Natural-language false positives are bounded by the same logic that protects Bearer: an English sentence containing "Basic" cannot produce 16+ contiguous chars from the base64 alphabet because words are separated by whitespace. The `_looks_like_secret(candidate, is_assignment=True)` filter at the second layer (`min_categories=1`) catches any token-shaped string that does happen to follow a `basic`-prefixed natural-language passage — the same defence Bearer already relies on.

**Tests added:** `tests/test_sentinel_secret_scanner_basic_auth_drift.py` — 32 tests across 10 categories:
  1. Per-case attribution PoCs (5 case variants × long-mixed body) — proves attribution-drift closes for every RFC-7235-conforming Basic literal.
  2. Per-case silent-undetection PoCs (4 case variants × short body) — proves short-body credentials (below entropy fallback's 24-char minimum) gain attribution.
  3. Per-case all-letter body PoCs (4 case variants × 40-char all-letter body) — proves `candidate.isalpha()` skip bypass closes.
  4. Negative cases (7 natural-language passages) — proves false-positive surface is bounded.
  5. Cross-detector ordering regression guard — proves JWT in Bearer header still yields JWT attribution (the `_KNOWN_TOKENS` matching runs BEFORE auth-scheme detectors).
  6. Bearer regression guard — proves canonical Bearer continues to detect after the refactor.
  7. Compiled-regex invariant — pins `_BASIC_AUTH_RE.flags & re.IGNORECASE` programmatically.
  8. End-to-end emission-shape inventory (6 real-world emission patterns: curl debug logs, JSON fixtures, YAML config, Python dicts).
  9. Masking-contract test — proves the raw base64 body never appears unmasked in finding output (mirrors Bearer's masking-contract test).
 10. Decoded-credential demonstration — documents the threat-model rationale (the body decodes back to `admin:password`, distinct from opaque Bearer).

**Learning:** The HTTP-auth-scheme detector family is a closed enumeration per the RFC 7235 §2.1 case-insensitivity contract. Pinning every detector in a shared `_AUTH_SCHEME_DETECTORS` table with a uniform processing path (single helper `_scan_auth_scheme_credentials`) defeats the drift mode where one detector gets the `(?i)` flag and a sibling does not. **The structural invariant for future auth-scheme detectors: any new HTTP-auth-scheme literal added MUST be a `_AUTH_SCHEME_DETECTORS` entry, NOT a separate `_KNOWN_TOKENS` entry or inline `_scan_content` loop.** The `_KNOWN_TOKENS` table is for VENDOR-prefixed tokens (canonical issuer prefix anchors the credential to its revocation flow — `ghp_`, `glpat-`, `xkeysib-`, etc.); the `_AUTH_SCHEME_DETECTORS` table is for IETF-RFC-defined auth-scheme literals (canonical HTTP authentication scheme anchors the credential to its RFC-defined recovery semantics — `Bearer` is opaque per RFC 6750, `Basic` is reversible base64 per RFC 7617). The closing-checklist invariant for future Sentinel rounds: every detector regex whose anchor is an HTTP-RFC-defined auth-scheme literal MUST be added to `_AUTH_SCHEME_DETECTORS` (NOT `_KNOWN_TOKENS`), inherit the `(?i)` inline flag per RFC 7235 §2.1, and route through `_scan_auth_scheme_credentials`. The 16+ char body-alphabet structural disambiguator + the `is_assignment=True` `_looks_like_secret` filter together prevent natural-language false positives without per-detector bikeshedding.

**Next-round candidates (named-but-deferred):** **Digest Auth detector** (`(?i)Digest\s+username=...`) — RFC 7616 multi-field challenge-response auth; the credential is split across the `nonce` / `response` / `cnonce` fields, so a single-regex anchor doesn't fully capture the secret — context-aware extraction needed, deferred pending body-structure-aware detector framework. **GitHub `Token` scheme detector** (`(?i)token\s+[A-Za-z0-9_\-]{36,}`) — GitHub's older API alias for Bearer (`Authorization: token ghp_...`); the embedded GitHub PAT (`ghp_`-prefixed) already matches `_KNOWN_TOKENS` via the `ghp_` regex, so the `token` scheme detector is attribution-only for opaque non-GitHub tokens that happen to use the alias. **SPNEGO detector** (`(?i)Negotiate\s+[A-Za-z0-9+/=]{50,}`) — RFC 4559 Kerberos via SPNEGO; the body is a base64-encoded GSSAPI token, body lengths typically much longer than Basic Auth (200+ chars per Kerberos ticket). **HOBA detector** (`(?i)HOBA\s+...`) — RFC 7486 HTTP Origin-Bound Auth; rare in practice but a complete auth-scheme enumeration would include it.

**Marker:** SENTINEL_BASIC_AUTH_DRIFT.

## 2026-05-16 - Default Env-File Candidate Symlink-Following Bypass: `_default_env_file_candidates` Skipped Symlink-Aware Resolution For Its Three *Built-In* Default Paths (`<base_dir>/.env`, `<base_dir>/data/secrets.env`, `<base_dir>/config/secrets.env`) — A Hostile PR Landing A Symlink At Any Of The Three Documented Default Paths Pointing Outside `base_dir` Was A Single-Precondition Arbitrary-File-Read Primitive Whose Blast Radius Equalled The `WIEN_OEPNV_ENV_FILES` Bypass (KEY=VALUE Pairs From `/etc/environment` / `~/.aws/credentials` Loaded Into `os.environ`) But With Half The Preconditions

**Vulnerability:** Sibling-drift closure of the `WIEN_OEPNV_ENV_FILES` symlink-following bypass (the previous entry in this journal). The pre-fix `_default_env_file_candidates` applied `Path.resolve(strict=False)` + `relative_to(base_dir)` containment ONLY to the env-controlled `WIEN_OEPNV_ENV_FILES` extras; the THREE built-in default paths (`<base_dir>/.env`, `<base_dir>/data/secrets.env`, `<base_dir>/config/secrets.env`) were kept as bare lexical `base_dir / "..."` Path objects with NO symlink-aware check. The bare lexical Path opens its target via `path.open` (which follows symlinks) so a symlink planted at any default location pointing OUTSIDE `base_dir` — e.g. `<base_dir>/.env -> /etc/environment`, `<base_dir>/data/secrets.env -> ~/.aws/credentials`, `<base_dir>/config/secrets.env -> /proc/self/environ` — turned every default candidate into an arbitrary-file-read primitive. Pre-fix `load_env_file(<symlink>)` opened the link (`path.exists()` and `path.is_file()` both follow symlinks), `read_capped_text` followed the link to the outside target (capped at `MAX_ENV_FILE_BYTES`), `_parse_env_file` parsed every `KEY=VALUE` line, and the loader wrote each variable into `os.environ` (`override=False` default). End-to-end PoC verified pre-fix across all THREE default paths simultaneously: `SENTINEL_DEFAULT_TRIPLE_DOT_ENV_LEAK` + `SENTINEL_DEFAULT_TRIPLE_DATA_SECRETS_LEAK` + `SENTINEL_DEFAULT_TRIPLE_CONFIG_SECRETS_LEAK` from three distinct outside-base files all landed in `os.environ` in a single `load_default_env_files()` call. **Severity MEDIUM** — the single-precondition shape is meaningfully different from the env-controlled round which needed TWO preconditions (filesystem planting + env var injection). The default-candidate branch needs only ONE precondition: filesystem write to any of the three publicly-documented default paths via (a) a hostile PR submitted against the repository — Git tracks symlinks as files with the `120000` mode, and merging the PR lands the link in `main` where every subsequent cron tick re-materialises it; (b) a pre-compromised CI runner or multi-tenant shared workspace where another tenant's job leaves a symlink at the documented default path; (c) a misconfigured development environment that accidentally generates a symlink pointing outside the project tree. Code review may not flag a new symlink as suspicious unless reviewers specifically inspect the mode bits — git diff UI shows symlinks as ordinary text files with the link target as the file content, easily mistaken for a tiny config snippet.

**Learning:** The structural lesson is that when a security defence is added to ONE branch of a multi-branch function, every sibling branch that hits the SAME downstream sink must be audited in lockstep. The previous round (`WIEN_OEPNV_ENV_FILES` bypass) added `Path.resolve(strict=False)` + `relative_to(base_dir)` to the env-controlled branch but left the default-candidate branch as bare lexical Path objects — same downstream sink (`load_env_file` → `read_capped_text` → `_parse_env_file` → `os.environ`), same blast radius, half the preconditions. The asymmetry was non-obvious because the default candidates LOOKED safe by construction (`base_dir / ".env"` is lexically inside `base_dir`, so why would a containment check matter?) — but the lexical-vs-resolved distinction the previous round established is precisely the gap: `<base_dir>/.env` is lexically inside `base_dir` regardless of whether the filesystem entry is a regular file or a symlink to `/etc/environment`. **Any path-containment defence at a security boundary must apply uniformly to every path that flows into the same sink** — the sink (here: `load_env_file`) is the canonical "I will follow symlinks" point; every path reaching the sink must have been resolved + checked. The fix extracts the resolve-and-check logic into a single `_resolve_within_base(candidate, base_dir)` helper that BOTH branches route through. Pinning a helper rather than parallel inline checks defeats the drift mode that produced this very vulnerability: future maintenance that touches one branch cannot silently bypass the defence on the other. Mathematical invariant: a function that returns candidates flowing into a symlink-aware sink must define a "candidate validity" predicate that is closed under composition — every candidate-producing branch must hit the SAME predicate, NOT a parallel inline approximation.

**Prevention:** Extract every path-containment defence into a named helper (here: `_resolve_within_base`) so every candidate-producing branch routes through one canonical implementation. The structural invariant test `test_default_env_file_candidates_routes_through_resolver` in `tests/test_sentinel_default_env_file_symlink_bypass.py` greps the function source for the helper name and fails if a future refactor drops the routing — pinning the routing prevents the parallel-inline regression that produced this round. Companion PoC test exercises all three default candidate paths (parametrised) plus a triple-simultaneous-leak scenario verifying that NO subset of the three planted symlinks leaks into `os.environ`. Future audits should walk `git grep -nE 'def _.*candidates' src/` to enumerate every candidate-list builder and verify each candidate flows through the canonical resolver. Generalisation: when an existing security helper is extracted from one branch, audit every sibling branch that hits the same downstream sink — the audit grep is `git grep -nE 'load_env_file\(' src/` for this specific sink; future audits of new sink families should adapt the pattern. The new `_resolve_within_base` helper also serves as a single point of audit: any future change to the containment semantics (e.g. tightening the strict=False default, adding a per-component symlink hop limit, switching from `relative_to` to a stricter prefix-bytes check) propagates to every caller automatically.

## 2026-05-16 - `WIEN_OEPNV_ENV_FILES` Symlink-Following Bypass: `_default_env_file_candidates` Used `os.path.abspath` (Lexical-Only Normalisation) Instead of `Path.resolve()` (Symlink-Aware) — A Symlink Planted Inside `base_dir` Pointing OUTSIDE (e.g. `<base_dir>/decoy.env -> /etc/environment`) Passed the `relative_to(base_dir)` Containment Check And Let `load_env_file` Read Arbitrary Filesystem Locations Via The Env-Controlled `WIEN_OEPNV_ENV_FILES` Path List
**Vulnerability:** `src/utils/env.py:_default_env_file_candidates` accepted `WIEN_OEPNV_ENV_FILES` entries by resolving them via `Path(os.path.abspath(candidate))` and checking containment via `relative_to(base_dir)`. `os.path.abspath` performs ONLY lexical normalisation (joins relative paths against `os.getcwd()` and resolves `.`/`..` parts via string manipulation); it does NOT follow symlinks. So a symlink planted inside `base_dir` pointing OUTSIDE `base_dir` — e.g. `<base_dir>/decoy.env -> /etc/environment`, `<base_dir>/decoy.env -> ~/.aws/credentials`, `<base_dir>/decoy.env -> /proc/self/environ` — passed the lexical containment check verbatim (the lexical path `<base_dir>/decoy.env` IS inside `base_dir`) while resolving to a filesystem destination FAR outside the intended boundary. Pre-fix `load_env_file(decoy.env)` then opened the symlink, followed it to the outside target, and `read_capped_text` read the target's bytes (up to `MAX_ENV_FILE_BYTES`). `_parse_env_file` parsed every `KEY=VALUE` line and the loader wrote each variable into `os.environ` (when `KEY` is not already set, per the `override=False` default). End-to-end PoC verified pre-fix: `STOLEN_SYMLINK_LEAK=pwned_value` from `/tmp/outside/stolen.env` landed in `os.environ` despite the symlink target lying entirely outside `base_dir`. **Severity MEDIUM** — defense-in-depth violation that breaks the explicit invariant the `relative_to(base_dir)` check was added to enforce; the two preconditions (filesystem planting + env var injection) are sometimes obtainable independently (a hostile PR that lands a symlink in `main` + a leaked CI env that sets `WIEN_OEPNV_ENV_FILES`).
**Learning:** The canonical path-containment pattern across `src/` already uses `Path.resolve()` consistently at the symlink-aware boundary: `read_secret` (`src/utils/env.py:188-189`) calls `Path(cred_dir).resolve()` then `(base_dir / name).resolve()` before `relative_to`; `validate_path` (`src/feed/config.py:211`) calls `path.resolve()` before iterating the allow-listed bases. `_default_env_file_candidates` was the **outlier sibling** that used `os.path.abspath` — the lexical-only sibling primitive that looks shape-equivalent but breaks the symlink invariant. The asymmetry is non-obvious: both produce absolute, normalised paths, but only `.resolve()` traverses symlinks. Any `relative_to(base_dir)` check at a SECURITY boundary (where the question is "where on the filesystem does this path actually point?") MUST resolve symlinks; only `.resolve()` answers that question. `os.path.abspath` answers a DIFFERENT question ("what does this path string look like when normalised lexically?") which is correct for thread-lock-key normalisation (`src/utils/locking.py:178`) but wrong for filesystem-boundary checks. The mathematical invariant: `Path.resolve()` is the unique canonical containment primitive — any sibling site that needs to answer "is this path actually inside the allowed sub-tree?" must use it. Audit grep for the dual: `git grep -nE 'os\.path\.abspath\(' src/` enumerates every site to inspect. `src/utils/files.py:safe_path_join` also uses `os.path.abspath` but is reached via the `_cache_file` regex-constrained provider name (`^[a-zA-Z0-9_-]+$`), so the symlink-planting precondition reduces to filesystem write access to `_CACHE_DIR` (game-over already) — the env.py site is the higher-priority closure because the env-controlled list IS the attack surface.
**Prevention:** Replace `Path(os.path.abspath(candidate))` with `candidate.resolve(strict=False)` at every env-controlled / network-controlled / user-controlled path-containment boundary. The `strict=False` argument preserves the pre-fix behaviour for non-existent paths (the lexical part resolves and the missing tail is appended) while still following every existing symlink hop along the path. Catch `OSError` (symlink loops via `ELOOP`, I/O errors) and `RuntimeError` (CPython infinite-loop guard on some Python versions) so the boundary fails closed — the secure default for any filesystem state that prevents full resolution. The companion `tests/test_sentinel_env_file_symlink_bypass.py` pins seven attack scenarios (direct symlink-to-outside, nested-symlink-chain, symlink-loop, pathsep-mixed legitimate-and-bypass, absolute-path-outside regression, `../` traversal regression, legitimate-symlink-staying-inside-base acceptance) PLUS a structural invariant test that fails any future refactor that re-introduces an `os.path.abspath(...)` call at the candidate boundary. Future audits should walk `git grep -nE 'os\.path\.abspath\([^)]*candidate' src/` to enumerate any drift sibling that creeps back in.

## 2026-05-16 - Entropy Heuristic Long-Base64 Detection Gap: `_looks_like_secret` Uniqueness Floor `len(set) >= len // 4` Was Mathematically Unsatisfiable For Multi-Hundred-Char Base64 Credentials (AWS STS Session Tokens, GCP Service-Account Bodies, Long Opaque OAuth Tokens) Whose Alphabet Plateaus At ~64 Distinct Chars While The Requirement Demanded 71+
**Vulnerability:** `src/utils/secret_scanner.py:_looks_like_secret` rejected any candidate whose distinct-character count fell below `max(6, len(candidate) // 4)`. For candidates longer than ~256 chars whose alphabet is the base64 ceiling (`[A-Za-z0-9+/=_-]` — at most 65 unique characters total), the `len // 4` requirement is mathematically impossible: a 287-char token has at most 65 unique chars but needs 71+ to pass. The gate applies to BOTH the entropy fallback (`_HIGH_ENTROPY_RE` → `_looks_like_secret(is_assignment=False)`) AND the assignment heuristic (`_SENSITIVE_ASSIGN_RE` → `_looks_like_secret(is_assignment=True)`), so a real-world AWS STS Session Token leaked in `.env`, log lines, JSON fixtures, or any plaintext context was **silently undetected entirely** — no finding produced by ANY detection branch, CI gate passes, credential commits to `main` history, and remains exploitable for the natural session-token validity window (15 min to 12 h depending on the originating session, long enough for credential pivoting via `iam:CreateAccessKey` or other privilege-escalation flows). The gap generalises to every long-base64 credential class: GCP service-account JSON `private_key` body (covered structurally by `_PEM_RE` for the `-----BEGIN PRIVATE KEY-----` block, but the bare-body shape was uncovered), Auth0 / Microsoft Graph access tokens > 256 chars, custom-IdP opaque tokens, and any future vendor token whose canonical format exceeds the breakpoint. **Severity HIGH** for the AWS-session-token case — silent CI gate bypass on a credential class with multi-hour validity and full IAM-scope blast radius.
**Learning:** The previous entropy-heuristic rounds (`_BEARER_RE` case-insensitivity, `xoxd-` uniform-body, etc.) all focused on the *category-count* axis (`min_categories=2`); none audited the *unique-character-count* axis. The two axes fail differently: the category-count gap silently undetects uniform-class bodies regardless of length; the uniqueness-count gap silently undetects diverse bodies *only above a length breakpoint* that ties to the alphabet ceiling. Any future detector design must check BOTH axes when reasoning about the entropy fallback's coverage envelope. The 32-char floor in the post-fix uniqueness gate is the breakpoint at which the alphabet-ceiling-bounded ratio diverges from the original `len // 4` ratio (`alphabet_size ≈ 64`, so `len // 4 > 32` at `len > 128`). The mathematical relationship `unique_chars <= min(len, alphabet_size)` is the load-bearing invariant: any uniqueness gate must be capped at `alphabet_size` to remain satisfiable across the realistic length range.
**Prevention:** When designing entropy-based detection heuristics, explicitly model the alphabet-ceiling constraint. For any candidate that matches an alphabet-limited regex (`[A-Za-z0-9+/=_-]{N,}`, `[a-f0-9]{N,}`, `[0-9]{N,}`), the uniqueness requirement should be `min(max(6, len // 4), alphabet_size)`. Audit grep: `git grep -nE 'len\(set\(.*\)\)\s*<' src/` enumerates every uniqueness-gated heuristic. Add a parametrised regression test that exercises strings of various lengths (24, 100, 287, 500, 1000) with the alphabet-bounded unique-char count to verify the cap holds across the long-tail. The fix shape (`max(6, min(len(candidate) // 4, 32))`) preserves behaviour for short candidates (length 24-127, where `len // 4` dominates) and only loosens the requirement for longer candidates where the alphabet ceiling makes the original ratio mathematically impossible. False-positive surface is bounded by the contiguous-match requirement of `_HIGH_ENTROPY_RE` (natural-language text breaks at punctuation) and the `_is_binary` filter that already skips committed binary files; remaining false-positive class (long base64 data URIs inline in HTML/Markdown) is addressable via `.secret-scan-ignore` if it materialises.

## 2026-05-16 - Secret Scanner Drift Round 15: Slack Workflow Token Family Closure (`xoxe-` / `xoxe.xoxb-` / `xoxe.xoxp-` V2 Token Rotation Refresh + `xoxc-` Browser Session + `xoxd-` Cookie Session) — The Three Adjacent-Prefix Candidates Round 14's Closing Checklist Explicitly Named-But-Deferred; Pre-Fix `xoxd-<uniform-body>` Was Silently Undetected Entirely (Entropy Fallback's `min_categories=2` Rejects Single-Class Bodies, `_KNOWN_TOKENS` Has No Entry, Assignment Heuristic Requires Sensitive Variable Name) And `xoxe-`/`xoxc-` Bare Leaks Lost Slack-Specific Issuer Attribution To Generic "Hochentropischer Token-String" Findings

**Vulnerability:** Round 14 (PR #1520, AWS ABIA prefix) closed the AWS credential-prefix family at 4-of-4 and explicitly named the next-round adjacent-prefix candidates: **Slack workflow tokens (`xoxe-` refresh, `xoxc-` browser, `xoxd-` cookie/session — adjacent-PREFIX candidates if the Slack family expands beyond the four already covered).** The pre-Round-15 Slack family covered four prefixes (`xoxb-` Bot / `xoxp-` User / `xoxa-` OAuth / `xoxr-` Legacy Refresh) and three canonical Slack token prefixes remained outside `_KNOWN_TOKENS`: the modern V2 token-rotation refresh format (`xoxe-` direct + `xoxe.xoxb-`/`xoxe.xoxp-` chained), the browser session token (`xoxc-`), and the cookie session token (`xoxd-`).

A leaked Slack workflow token fails the canonical detection branches in `_scan_content`:

1. **`_PEM_RE`** — no PEM markers; no match.

2. **`_KNOWN_TOKENS`** — pre-fix no entry covers `xoxe-`/`xoxc-`/`xoxd-`; no match.

3. **`_AWS_ID_RE`** — wrong issuer family; no match.

4. **`_BEARER_RE`** — bare Slack tokens lack the `Bearer ` prefix; no match.

5. **`_SENSITIVE_ASSIGN_RE`** — only fires when the variable name carries a sensitive keyword. For bare token leaks in log lines, JSON fixtures without sensitive keys, comments, documentation snippets, or arbitrary text — NO match.

6. **`_HIGH_ENTROPY_RE`** — the body alphabet `[0-9a-zA-Z\-]` lies inside the entropy alphabet `[A-Za-z0-9+/=_-]`, so the body span matches generically as `Hochentropischer Token-String` — BUT this **loses the Slack-specific issuer attribution** (revocation flow at api.slack.com/apps/<app>/oauth for `xoxe-` rotation; https://slack.com/account/sessions for `xoxc-`/`xoxd-` browser-cookie auth — distinct from every other vendor's). For uniform-character-class bodies (all-lowercase / all-uppercase / all-digit, common for hash-derived or poorly-seeded RNG tokens), `_looks_like_secret` requires `min_categories=2` and returns `False` — the credential is silently undetected entirely (verified pre-fix for `xoxd-` with 32 uppercase chars). The chained `xoxe.xoxb-<body>` shape additionally trips up the strict bare `xoxb-` regex (which requires a `[0-9]{10,}-[0-9]{10,}-[a-zA-Z0-9]{24}` body that the rotation shape `1-<base64body>` does NOT match) — so no detection branch ever fires for the canonical Slack V2 token-rotation refresh token in the wild.

**Threat model:** A real-world Slack workflow token (V2 rotation refresh token from `oauth.v2.access` with `grant_type=refresh_token`, browser/cookie session token extracted from Slack web client DevTools by unofficial tools like slackdump / slack_cleaner) leaked alongside non-sensitive context (debug log, JSON fixture, hostile-PR fragment, hand-typed documentation snippet) slips past the secret scanner CI gate entirely or lands without issuer-specific attribution. Per-prefix blast radius:

  * **`xoxe-` / `xoxe.xoxb-` / `xoxe.xoxp-`** (Token Rotation Refresh): holder can mint fresh `xoxb-`/`xoxp-` access tokens with the rotation chain's identity and scopes until revocation. Slack's V2 rotation gives 12-hour access-token TTL but refresh tokens are long-lived (multi-month), so a leaked refresh token grants persistent access until manual revocation via api.slack.com/apps/<app>/oauth > Reinstall App. Multi-month blast radius.

  * **`xoxc-`** (Browser Session Token): full user-level session auth with **no scope restrictions** — equivalent to the user being logged in via the web client. An attacker holding `xoxc-` can browse every DM / private channel / sensitive file the user can see, post messages as the user, and exfiltrate the workspace's full message history via cookie-authenticated `/api/conversations.list` / `conversations.history` endpoints. Revocation flow at https://slack.com/account/sessions ("Sign out all other sessions") — distinct from the OAuth-app revocation flow.

  * **`xoxd-`** (Cookie Session Token): companion to `xoxc-`, the `d` cookie value from the browser session. Used in conjunction with `xoxc-` for direct browser-style API calls. A leak grants the same SESSION-LEVEL access as `xoxc-`; both tokens typically leak together via DevTools cookie extraction.

**Sites closed (3 new `_KNOWN_TOKENS` entries):**

  1. `src/utils/secret_scanner.py:_KNOWN_TOKENS` — `Slack Token Rotation Refresh Token gefunden` matching `(?<![A-Za-z0-9])xoxe(?:-|\.xox[bp]-)[0-9a-zA-Z\-]{20,}(?![A-Za-z0-9])`. Covers BOTH the direct shape (`xoxe-<body>`) AND the chained shape (`xoxe.xoxb-<body>` / `xoxe.xoxp-<body>`) per https://api.slack.com/authentication/rotation. The non-capturing alternation `(?:-|\.xox[bp]-)` between the prefix and body handles both shapes in a single regex while keeping the body strict (`[0-9a-zA-Z\-]` — no dots in the post-prefix span).
  2. `src/utils/secret_scanner.py:_KNOWN_TOKENS` — `Slack Browser Session Token gefunden` matching `(?<![A-Za-z0-9])xoxc-[0-9a-zA-Z\-]{20,}(?![A-Za-z0-9])`. Permissive 20+ char body mirrors the existing `xoxa-`/`xoxr-` shape (Slack session tokens use a similar structured-with-dashes format).
  3. `src/utils/secret_scanner.py:_KNOWN_TOKENS` — `Slack Cookie Session Token gefunden` matching `(?<![A-Za-z0-9])xoxd-[0-9a-zA-Z\-]{20,}(?![A-Za-z0-9])`. Same permissive body shape; mutually exclusive prefix.

**Placement invariant:** All three new entries are placed BEFORE the existing `xoxb-` / `xoxp-` / `xoxa-` / `xoxr-` entries in the `_KNOWN_TOKENS` list. This ordering matters because `_scan_content` iterates `_KNOWN_TOKENS` in list order and `is_covered` suppresses overlapping later matches — so the larger `xoxe.xoxb-<body>` / `xoxe.xoxp-<body>` chained span correctly anchors at the rotation attribution, suppressing any inner-span match the permissive `xoxa-`/`xoxr-` regexes might claim on the post-`xoxe.` portion. The strict bare `xoxb-`/`xoxp-` regexes don't match the rotation body shape anyway (`1-<base64body>` ≠ `[0-9]{10,}-[0-9]{10,}-[a-zA-Z0-9]{24,32}`), so the canonical bare tokens still receive their canonical attribution.

**Severity:** **HIGH-MEDIUM** — silent-undetection for uniform-body `xoxd-` / `xoxe-` (the high-severity branch where the credential never triggers a CI alert and remains exploitable until manual audit) + attribution-drift for mixed-body `xoxe-`/`xoxc-`/`xoxd-` (medium-severity branch where the entropy fallback caught the body span but lost the Slack-specific issuer attribution that determines which revocation playbook applies). The chained `xoxe.xoxb-`/`xoxe.xoxp-` shape is the worst-case real-world emission (Slack's canonical V2 rotation format), and the strict bare `xoxb-`/`xoxp-` regexes don't match it — pre-fix EVERY V2 rotation-refresh-token leak in the wild bypassed the Slack detector entirely.

**Fix:** Three new `_KNOWN_TOKENS` entries appended to `src/utils/secret_scanner.py`, positioned right before the existing `xoxb-` entry so `is_covered` anchors the chained-shape attribution correctly.

Companion `tests/test_sentinel_secret_scanner_drift_round15.py` (26 tests): per-prefix detection PoCs (direct `xoxe-`, chained `xoxe.xoxb-`, chained `xoxe.xoxp-`, `xoxc-`, `xoxd-`), per-prefix plaintext-context PoCs (log line / JSON fixture without sensitive variable name — silent-undetection branch demonstration), per-prefix negative cases (short bodies < 20 chars correctly NOT matched, mid-word prefixes correctly NOT matched via lookbehind anchor), regression guards for ALL four existing Slack prefixes (xoxb- / xoxp- / xoxa- / xoxr- retain canonical attribution), seven-prefix coexistence test (every Slack prefix produces a distinct finding with correct attribution), cross-vendor boundary regression (GitHub PAT / GitLab PAT / Stripe / Google API keys not mis-attributed to Slack patterns), inventory invariants (source-grep enforces `xoxe`/`xoxc-`/`xoxd-` presence in `_KNOWN_TOKENS` — future regressions that drop a pattern fail the invariant immediately), per-prefix masking-contract tests (raw value never appears unredacted in `Finding.match`), and the silent-undetection-via-uniform-body PoC that directly exercises the `_looks_like_secret` `min_categories=2` rejection path that the entropy fallback hits for all-uppercase bodies. Every test fails pre-fix and passes post-fix.

**Learning:** **The Slack token-prefix family is enumerated, NOT pattern-derived — every Slack token prefix MUST be explicitly listed in `_KNOWN_TOKENS` because the issuer-specific attribution is the determinant for which Slack revocation flow applies (api.slack.com/apps/* for OAuth-app tokens; slack.com/account/sessions for browser-cookie session tokens; a workspace's "Reinstall App" flow for rotation chains). Each prefix has a structurally distinct revocation playbook — losing the prefix-specific attribution to a generic high-entropy finding silently degrades the incident-response triage.** The seven Slack prefixes per Slack's published token taxonomy:

  * `xoxb-` (Bot User Token) — covered Round 1; strict shape; OAuth-app reinstall to revoke.
  * `xoxp-` (User Token) — covered Round 1; strict shape; OAuth-app reinstall to revoke.
  * `xoxa-` (OAuth App Configuration Token) — covered Round 1; permissive shape; api.slack.com/apps/ rotation.
  * `xoxr-` (Legacy Refresh Token) — covered Round 1; permissive shape; api.slack.com/apps/ rotation.
  * `xoxe-` / `xoxe.xoxb-` / `xoxe.xoxp-` (V2 Rotation Refresh) — Round 15 (THIS), api.slack.com/apps/<app>/oauth > Reinstall App.
  * `xoxc-` (Browser Session) — Round 15 (THIS), slack.com/account/sessions sign-out.
  * `xoxd-` (Cookie Session) — Round 15 (THIS), slack.com/account/sessions sign-out.

The closing-checklist invariant: every token whose vendor publishes a distinct revocation flow MUST receive its vendor-specific attribution; reliance on the entropy fallback's generic finding silently degrades the IR triage even when the entropy alphabet does happen to match the body. The chained-shape `xoxe.xoxb-`/`xoxe.xoxp-` case demonstrates the **ordering invariant**: when a new prefix's body region overlaps with an existing detector's body region, the new entry MUST be placed BEFORE the existing one in `_KNOWN_TOKENS` to ensure `is_covered`-based suppression yields the correct (more-specific) attribution. The Round-12 Shopify family closure already established this ordering invariant for `shpat_`/`shpss_`/`shppa_`/`shpca_` — Round 15 confirms the invariant generalises to the rotation-chain encoding shape.

**Next-round candidates (named-but-deferred):** The Slack issuer family is now closed at 7-of-7 documented prefixes. The following adjacent next-round candidates apply the same enumeration-not-derivation contract: **Basic Auth detector** (`(?i)Basic\s+[A-Za-z0-9+/=]{16,}` per RFC 7617) — named by the 2026-05-15 Bearer case-sensitivity round; closes the second auth-scheme literal in the RFC 7235 family. **GCP service account key** (typically a JSON file with an embedded `-----BEGIN PRIVATE KEY-----` block) — covered structurally by `_PEM_RE`; bucket-(b) deferred. **Azure SAS tokens** (`?sv=YYYY-MM-DD&...&sig=<base64>`) — adjacent context candidate, deferred pending query-string-aware extraction. **Cloudflare API token** (`<40 char alphanumeric>` — bucket-(b), no canonical prefix). **Azure storage account key** (`<88 char base64>` — bucket-(a) candidate pending shape-disambiguator analysis to avoid false-positive noise). **Heroku Platform API key** (UUID format — bucket-(b), shape would collide with operator-named UUIDs without context). **Datadog API key** (`<32 lowercase hex>` — bucket-(b), no canonical prefix; shape collides with operator-named hashes).

**Marker:** SENTINEL_SLACK_WORKFLOW_TOKEN_DRIFT.

## 2026-05-16 - Secret Scanner Drift Round 14: AWS STS Service Bearer Token (`ABIA<16>`) — The Fourth Credential Prefix In The AWS 4-Char Unique-Identifier Family That `_AWS_ID_RE` Explicitly Enumerated As Named-But-Uncovered (Round 13 Closing Checklist Listed Only `AKIA`/`ASIA`/`ACCA`); Pre-Fix Bare `ABIA<16>` Tokens Slipped Through Every Detection Branch (Entropy Fallback's 24-Char Minimum Rejects The 20-Char ABIA Shape; `_AWS_ID_RE` Enumerates Only 3 Of 4 Prefixes; Assignment Heuristic Loses AWS-Specific Attribution)

**Vulnerability:** The `_AWS_ID_RE = re.compile(r"(?<![A-Za-z0-9])(AKIA|ASIA|ACCA)[A-Z0-9]{16}(?![A-Za-z0-9])")` regex in `src/utils/secret_scanner.py` enumerates only **three of the four** AWS 4-character credential prefixes documented in the AWS IAM "Unique identifiers" reference. The missing fourth — `ABIA` (AWS STS service bearer token, issued by `sts:GetServiceBearerToken` for service-to-service authentication on behalf of an AWS user) — follows the identical 4+16=20 char format but is silently excluded from the enumeration. Per gitleaks / trufflehog / detect-secrets / aws-secret-detector default rules, ABIA is the fourth AWS credential prefix that grants direct API access (alongside AKIA/ASIA/ACCA); only the AWS-non-credential prefixes (AGPA group, AIDA IAM user, AIPA EC2 instance profile, ANPA SNS access point, ANVA account, APKA public key, AROA role, ARPA resource, ASCA certificate) are deliberately omitted as identifiers-not-credentials.

A leaked `ABIA<16>` token in committed source / log artefacts / CI debug snippets / hostile-PR fragments fails ALL six detection branches in `_scan_content`:

1. **`_PEM_RE`** — no PEM markers; no match.

2. **`_KNOWN_TOKENS`** — pre-fix no entry covered `ABIA`; no match.

3. **`_AWS_ID_RE`** — pre-fix the regex enumerates only `(AKIA|ASIA|ACCA)`; `ABIA` is silently excluded.

4. **`_BEARER_RE`** — requires the literal `Bearer ` keyword before the body; a bare ABIA token without that prefix does not match.

5. **`_SENSITIVE_ASSIGN_RE`** — only fires when the *variable name* carries a sensitive keyword (`key`/`secret`/`token`/etc.). For bare token leaks in log lines, JSON fixtures without sensitive keys, comments, documentation snippets, or arbitrary text — no match.

6. **`_HIGH_ENTROPY_RE`** — requires `{24,}` chars from `[A-Za-z0-9+/=_-]`. The ABIA token format is exactly **20 chars** (4-char prefix + 16-char body). The 20-char shape falls **below** the 24-char entropy threshold; no match.

Net result pre-fix: a bare `ABIAV2EXAMPLE12345AB` leaked into a log line, JSON fixture, error message, comment, or documentation snippet is **silently undetected** by every detection branch — the CI gate passes, the credential sits in the public repository indefinitely, and the issuing user's full AWS scope (the bearer token's authorized API access window) is exposed to every consumer. Even when the token IS embedded in a sensitive-keyword assignment (`AWS_BEARER_TOKEN = "ABIA…"`), the only finding emitted is the generic `Verdächtige Zuweisung eines potentiellen Secrets` — losing the AWS-specific issuer attribution that incident-response playbooks key off (revocation flow at IAM > STS service bearer token management — distinct from the IAM > Access keys flow used to revoke `AKIA` tokens).

Pre-fix PoC (verified):
```python
abia_token = 'ABIAV2EXAMPLE123ABCD'  # 20 chars, real AWS-credential format
content = f'2026-05-15T14:32:01Z DEBUG sts.client received bearer token {abia_token} for service auth'
_scan_content(content)  # → []  EMPTY — silently undetected
```

**Threat model:** A real-world AWS STS service bearer token (issued by `sts:GetServiceBearerToken` for any AWS service that supports bearer-token-based auth — AWS CodeCatalyst, IoT Device Defender, certain Bedrock APIs, federated workload-identity flows) leaked alongside non-sensitive context (CloudTrail debug log line, AWS SDK debug trace with `AWS_DEBUG=true`, hand-typed documentation example, hostile-PR fragment demonstrating service-to-service auth) slips past the secret scanner CI gate entirely. Bearer tokens carry whatever scope `sts:GetServiceBearerToken` was authorized to grant — typically the same scope as the issuing user's IAM policy, restricted to the target service. The high-severity case is the silent-undetection branch: a credential committed in any non-assignment context never triggers a CI alert, never gets rotated, and remains exploitable until the bearer token's TTL expires (typically 12 hours but can be extended via STS regional endpoints).

The 20-char shape exclusion from `_HIGH_ENTROPY_RE`'s 24-char minimum is the structural root cause — every AWS credential prefix family member is exactly 20 chars by AWS spec, so the explicit enumeration in `_AWS_ID_RE` is the *only* line of defence. Missing one prefix from that enumeration creates a complete detection-from-zero gap for that prefix family. Pre-fix every real-world ABIA emission shape (CloudTrail `ResponseElements.bearerToken`, AWS SDK retry/debug log line, JSON fixture with non-sensitive key, documentation example) bypassed the AWS detector. Post-fix every emission shape — across every context — yields the AWS-STS-Service-Bearer-Token-specific attribution.

**Sites closed (1 detector regex added to `_KNOWN_TOKENS`):**

  * `src/utils/secret_scanner.py:_KNOWN_TOKENS` — single `(re.compile(...), reason)` tuple appended to the table:
    ```python
    (
        re.compile(r"(?<![A-Za-z0-9])ABIA[A-Z0-9]{16}(?![A-Za-z0-9])"),
        "AWS STS Service Bearer Token gefunden",
    ),
    ```
    Strict `[A-Z0-9]{16}` body alphabet (uppercase + digits, mirroring `_AWS_ID_RE`) anchors against false positives on lowercase or mixed-case strings happening to start with `ABIA` (English words / placeholder identifiers). The `(?<![A-Za-z0-9])` lookbehind prevents matching mid-word occurrences. Distinct attribution from the generic `AWS Access Key ID gefunden` reason so the report identifies WHICH AWS credential type leaked and the operator can rotate via the correct STS revocation flow rather than the IAM access-key rotation flow.

The KNOWN_TOKENS processing branch runs **before** `_AWS_ID_RE` in `_scan_content`, so `is_covered` correctly anchors the ABIA detection at the more-specific issuer attribution and the existing AKIA/ASIA/ACCA matches via `_AWS_ID_RE` are unaffected (regression guards in the companion test confirm).

**Severity:** **HIGH** — detection-from-zero failure for an AWS credential prefix that grants direct API access. The 20-char format places ABIA tokens **below** the entropy fallback's 24-char minimum (every other AWS prefix in the family is also 20 chars but they are explicitly enumerated, so the enumeration is the only line of defence). Every bare ABIA leak in a non-assignment context is silently undetected; assignment-context leaks lose the AWS-specific issuer attribution. CodeQL / Semgrep do not flag this drift because the AWS unique-identifier prefix taxonomy is encoded in AWS documentation, not in any static-analysis catalogue.

**Fix:** Single-tuple addition to `_KNOWN_TOKENS`. Zero false-positive risk: the strict `[A-Z0-9]{16}` body alphabet rejects lowercase / mixed-case / hyphen / underscore content (every AWS-credential body is uppercase + digits per spec); the `(?<![A-Za-z0-9])` lookbehind prevents mid-word matches; the explicit 16-char body length matches the AWS spec exactly (no quantifier slop).

Companion `tests/test_sentinel_secret_scanner_drift_round14.py` (10 tests): plaintext-context PoC (silent-undetection branch), JSON-fixture PoC (no sensitive variable name), assignment-context PoC (attribution-drift branch), three negative cases (short body, lowercase body, mid-word ABIA), three regression guards (AKIA/ASIA/ACCA still receive canonical `AWS Access Key ID gefunden` attribution — no collision), and an inventory invariant pin (`ABIA` and `AWS STS Service Bearer Token` strings present in `secret_scanner.py` source — fails on regression to pre-fix shape).

**Learning:** **The AWS 4-character credential-prefix family is enumerated, NOT pattern-derived — every credential prefix MUST be explicitly listed in `_AWS_ID_RE` (or `_KNOWN_TOKENS`) because the 20-char AWS format falls below `_HIGH_ENTROPY_RE`'s 24-char minimum, so the enumeration is the only line of defence.** The four AWS credential prefixes per AWS IAM "Unique identifiers" reference are: `AKIA` (long-term IAM access key) + `ASIA` (STS short-term access key) + `ACCA` (context-specific credentials) + `ABIA` (STS service bearer token). The non-credential AWS prefixes (`AGPA` group, `AIDA` IAM user, `AIPA` EC2 instance profile, `ANPA` SNS access point, `ANVA` account, `APKA` public key, `AROA` role, `ARPA` resource, `ASCA` certificate) are deliberately omitted — they are identifiers-not-credentials and would create false-positive noise in policy/IAM documentation files without IR value. The closing-checklist invariant for future Sentinel rounds is: **any new vendor whose token format is shorter than 24 characters MUST be explicitly enumerated (cannot rely on the entropy fallback as a safety net).** This applies equally to short-format tokens like AWS prefixes (20 chars), some Twilio Auth Tokens (32 chars but one-category hex — would fail `min_categories=2`), and any future vendor with a `<short prefix><uniform body>` shape.

**Next-round candidates (named-but-deferred):** The AWS credential-prefix family is now closed at 4-of-4. The following adjacent next-round candidates apply the same enumeration-not-derivation contract: **GCP service account key** (typically a JSON file with a 24+ char private key fingerprint inside, but no canonical short prefix — covered structurally by `_PEM_RE` for the embedded `-----BEGIN PRIVATE KEY-----` block; bucket-(b) deferred). **Azure SAS tokens** (`?sv=YYYY-MM-DD&...&sig=<base64>` — adjacent context candidate, deferred pending query-string-aware extraction; the `&sig=` parameter body matches `_HIGH_ENTROPY_RE` generically but loses the Azure-specific issuer attribution that anchors revocation at the Storage Account access-keys page). **Cloudflare API token** (`<40 char alphanumeric>` — bucket-(b), no canonical prefix). **Azure storage account key** (`<88 char base64>` — covered by `_HIGH_ENTROPY_RE` structurally but loses Azure attribution; bucket-(a) candidate pending shape-disambiguator analysis to avoid false-positive noise). **Slack workflow tokens** (`xoxe-` refresh token, `xoxc-` browser token, `xoxd-` cookie/session token — adjacent-PREFIX candidates if the Slack family expands beyond the four already covered).

**Marker:** SENTINEL_AWS_ABIA_PREFIX_DRIFT.

## 2026-05-15 - Bearer Token Detector Case-Sensitivity Drift (RFC 7235 §2.1 Auth-Scheme Case-Insensitive Contract): `_BEARER_RE` Matched Only Canonical-Case `Bearer` Literal — A Leaked `BEARER <body>` / `bearer <body>` / `BeArEr <body>` Authorization-Header Fragment With a Uniform-Character-Class Body (All-Lowercase / All-Uppercase / All-Digit) Was SILENTLY UNDETECTED Entirely (Both Bearer-Specific AND Entropy-Fallback Branches), And a Mixed-Case Body Lost the Bearer-Token-Specific Issuer Attribution to a Generic "Hochentropischer Token-String" Finding

**Vulnerability:** The `_BEARER_RE = re.compile(r"Bearer\s+([A-Za-z0-9\-_.]{16,})")` regex in `src/utils/secret_scanner.py` matched the `Bearer` literal **case-sensitively**, but RFC 7235 §2.1 mandates that the HTTP auth-scheme component is matched **case-insensitively** by every conforming receiver.
